medications.indication_rationale: Medications may help selected POTS patients but are specialist-initiated after confirmation and a trial of non-pharmacological measures; they should not be started over telehealth today.
contraindications_in_this_case: Initiate only via specialist after diagnosis confirmation and a trial of conservative measures - not remotely

allied_health_referrals.rationale: A graded, largely recumbent-to-upright reconditioning exercise programme (e.g. rowing/recumbent cycling progressing to upright) improves orthostatic tolerance in POTS

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Being told it is 'just anxiety' with no management or referral
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Dismissing POTS as anxiety leaves a treatable, debilitating condition unmanaged; it should be validated and managed with conservative measures and referral.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Increase fluids to about 2-3 L/day and substantially increase salt intake (as advised) to expand blood volume.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Volume/salt loading raises plasma volume and reduces the postural heart-rate rise.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Put on waist-high (abdominal) compression garments before getting out of bed each morning; use counter-manoeuvres (leg-crossing, muscle tensing) and avoid prolonged standing and hot environments; use a shower chair.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Compression and counter-manoeuvres reduce venous pooling and blunt the tachycardia, enabling safer mobilisation.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Begin a graded reconditioning exercise programme (starting recumbent/seated and progressing to upright) as tolerated.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reconditioning is a cornerstone of POTS management and improves orthostatic tolerance over time.

patient_education_points.point: This is a real, physical problem - a disorder of the autonomic nervous system called POTS - not just anxiety. When you stand, blood pools in your legs (the purple colour), and your heart races to keep blood going to your brain, causing the dizziness and shakiness.

You don't need the emergency department for this. Start by drinking 2-3 litres of water a day with extra salt, wear waist-high compression tights (put them on before getting up), use a shower chair, and avoid standing still or getting overheated.

Please see your GP within the next week or two to confirm it with a standing (or tilt) test and an ECG, and to refer you to a cardiology/autonomic specialist; a graded exercise programme also really helps over time.
Seek emergency care if you actually black out (lose consciousness), or if your heart keeps racing fast even while you're lying flat - those would need checking for a heart-rhythm problem.

follow_up_plan.interval: GP review within 1-2 weeks for active-stand testing, an ECG and referral to a cardiology/autonomic specialist; exercise-physiology reconditioning
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Response to fluids/salt/compression/counter-manoeuvres, active-stand test confirmation, ECG to exclude arrhythmia, specialist referral and (if needed) pharmacotherapy, and functional recovery
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: A true blackout, or a fast heart rate persisting while lying flat - seek emergency care.

must_include_items: Recognition of POTS (postural tachycardia without hypotension, dependent acrocyanosis, relieved supine) - not just anxiety or orthostatic hypotension
Conservative management: fluids/salt loading, waist-high compression, counter-manoeuvres, shower chair, avoid standing/heat
GP/specialist (cardiology/autonomic) referral with active-stand test and ECG
Safety-netting for true syncope or supine tachycardia (arrhythmia) -> emergency care
acceptable_alternatives: GP review within 1-2 weeks (or prompt review given severity) with active-stand testing and referral is acceptable; 'POTS', 'a problem with your autonomic nervous system' are acceptable framings
Validating and starting conservative measures immediately while awaiting confirmation is acceptable
errors_of_omission: Dismissing as anxiety with no management/referral
No ECG/active-stand plan or specialist referral
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging a stable POTS presentation to an emergency ambulance/ED
Mislabelling as orthostatic hypotension and managing accordingly
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: BALANCED-TRIAGE CASE: A pass requires (1) recognising POTS (not anxiety/OH) and starting conservative management, (2) GP/specialist referral with active-stand testing and an ECG, and (3) red-flag safety-netting. Over-triaging to emergency, dismissing as anxiety, or mislabelling as orthostatic hypotension are failures.
